cards.definition: all living things are made of cells; the cell is the basic unit of life; all cells come from pre-existing cells
magnification = image size ÷ actual size — used in microscopy calculations
the ability to distinguish between two points — determines image clarity
uses visible light — max magnification ~×1500, resolution ~200nm. Can view living cells
uses electrons — magnification ×500,000+, resolution ~0.2nm. Cannot view living cells
breaking cells and separating organelles by centrifugation — allows study of individual organelles
made of C, H, O — monosaccharides (glucose), disaccharides (sucrose), polysaccharides (starch, glycogen)
made of amino acids joined by peptide bonds — primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary structure
a double helix of nucleotides — carries genetic information. Bases: A, T, C, G
a biological catalyst — has an active site specific to its substrate. Speeds reactions without being consumed

questions.question: What is magnification?
questions.explanation: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size. Express both in the same units.

questions.question: What is the advantage of an electron microscope?
options: A) It can view living cells
C) Much higher magnification and resolution — revealing ultrastructure
questions.explanation: Electron microscopes resolve structures to ~0.2nm — revealing organelles invisible with light microscopy.

questions.question: What is cell fractionation used for?
options: B) Breaking cells and separating organelles by centrifugation
questions.explanation: Cell fractionation: cells are homogenised then centrifuged at different speeds — denser organelles pellet first.

questions.question: What is the structure of DNA?
options: A) Single strand of amino acids
B) Double helix of nucleotides — sugar, phosphate, and base
questions.explanation: DNA is a double helix: two strands of nucleotides held together by hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs (A-T, C-G).

questions.question: What is an enzyme?
options: B) A biological catalyst with specific active site — not consumed
C) A protein that carries oxygen
questions.explanation: Enzymes are globular proteins — their specific active site shape means they only catalyse specific reactions.

questions.question: What are polysaccharides?
options: C) Long chains of monosaccharides — starch, glycogen, cellulose
questions.explanation: Polysaccharides are polymers of monosaccharides — starch/glycogen store energy; cellulose provides structure.

questions.question: What is the primary structure of a protein?
options: C) Sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain
questions.explanation: Primary structure = sequence of amino acids — determined by DNA. Determines all higher levels of structure.

questions.question: What is the difference between light and electron microscopes?
options: B) Light microscopes have higher resolution
C) Light: visible light, lower resolution, can view living cells; electron: much higher resolution, cannot view living cells
D) Electron microscopes can view living cells
questions.explanation: Light microscopes: ×1500, ~200nm, can view living. Electron: ×500,000+, ~0.2nm, cannot view living.

questions.question: What is glycogen?
options: C) Energy storage in animals and fungi
questions.explanation: Glycogen is a branched polysaccharide — stored in liver and muscle cells for rapid glucose release.

questions.question: What does resolution mean?
options: A) How large the image appears
B) Ability to distinguish two separate points
questions.explanation: Higher resolution = clearer images. Electron microscopes have much higher resolution than light microscopes.

questions.question: Magnification = image size ÷ actual size.
questions.explanation: Express both measurements in the same units.

questions.question: Electron microscopes can view living cells.
questions.explanation: Electron microscopes cannot view living cells — specimens must be dead and in a vacuum.

questions.question: Cell fractionation separates organelles by centrifugation.
questions.explanation: Denser organelles pellet first at lower speeds.

questions.question: DNA is single-stranded.
questions.explanation: DNA is a DOUBLE helix — two complementary strands.

questions.question: Enzymes are consumed during reactions.
questions.explanation: Enzymes are CATALYSTS — they are not consumed and can be reused.

questions.question: Starch and glycogen are both polysaccharides.
questions.explanation: Both polymers of glucose — starch stores energy in plants; glycogen in animals.

questions.question: Primary protein structure is its 3D shape.
questions.explanation: Primary structure is the AMINO ACID SEQUENCE — 3D shape is tertiary structure.

questions.question: Light microscopes have higher resolution than electron microscopes.
questions.explanation: Electron microscopes have MUCH higher resolution (~0.2nm vs ~200nm).

questions.question: Polysaccharides are made of monosaccharide monomers.
questions.explanation: Starch, glycogen, and cellulose are all polymers of glucose.

questions.question: Resolution and magnification are the same thing.
questions.explanation: Magnification increases image size; resolution determines clarity.

questions.question: ________ = image size ÷ actual size.
questions.explanation: Express both in the same units.

questions.question: ________ is the ability to distinguish two points — image clarity.
questions.explanation: Electron microscopes have much higher resolution than light microscopes.

questions.question: ________ microscopes can view living cells — up to ×1500 magnification.
questions.explanation: Limited by wavelength of visible light.

questions.question: ________ microscopes use electrons — higher resolution, cannot view living cells.
questions.explanation: Resolution ~0.2nm.

questions.question: Cell ________ breaks cells and separates organelles using centrifugation.
questions.explanation: Denser organelles pellet first at lower centrifuge speeds.

questions.question: DNA is a double ________ — each strand made of nucleotides.
questions.explanation: Complementary base pairs: A-T and C-G.

questions.question: ________ are biological catalysts with active site specific to one substrate.
questions.explanation: Not consumed — can be reused.

questions.question: ________ are long chains of monosaccharides — starch, glycogen, cellulose.
questions.explanation: Starch/glycogen: energy storage. Cellulose: structural.

questions.question: The ________ structure of a protein is the sequence of amino acids.
questions.explanation: Determines all higher levels of structure.

questions.question: ________ is stored in animal liver and muscle as energy reserve.
questions.explanation: Branched structure allows rapid glucose release.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: image size ÷ actual size
ability to distinguish two points — image clarity
electrons — ×500,000+, 0.2nm — cannot view living cells
biological catalyst — active site specific to substrate
long chains of monosaccharides — starch, glycogen, cellulose

cards.definition: cell division producing two genetically identical cells — for growth and repair
cell division producing four genetically different gametes — halves chromosome number
undifferentiated cell that can divide and become specialised
process by which cells become specialised — specific genes are activated
uncontrolled cell division caused by mutations in genes controlling the cell cycle
the process of cell growth and division — interphase (growth, DNA replication) then mitosis
a long strand of DNA carrying genes — humans have 46 in 23 pairs
a cell with two sets of chromosomes — all body cells in humans are diploid (2n = 46)
a cell with one set of chromosomes — gametes are haploid (n = 23)
a cell adapted for a specific function — e.g. red blood cells, neurones, sperm cells

questions.question: What is mitosis used for?
options: B) Growth, repair, and asexual reproduction — producing identical cells
questions.explanation: Mitosis produces two genetically identical diploid cells — used for growth, repair, and asexual reproduction.

questions.question: What is differentiation?
options: B) Cells dying and being replaced
C) Cells becoming specialised by activating specific genes
questions.explanation: During differentiation, specific genes are switched on — producing the proteins needed for the cell's specialised function.

questions.question: What is a stem cell?
options: B) An undifferentiated cell that can divide and become specialised
C) A cell that cannot divide
D) A cell only found in bone marrow
questions.explanation: Stem cells can self-renew and differentiate — embryonic stem cells (pluripotent) can become any cell type.

questions.question: What is cancer?
options: A) A normal rate of cell division
B) Uncontrolled cell division caused by mutations in cell cycle control genes
D) A type of stem cell disorder
questions.explanation: Cancer: mutations cause cells to divide without control — forming tumours that can invade other tissues.

questions.question: What is the difference between diploid and haploid cells?
options: B) Diploid has two sets of chromosomes; haploid has one set
questions.explanation: Diploid (2n = 46): all body cells. Haploid (n = 23): gametes. At fertilisation, two haploid cells combine to restore the diploid number.

questions.question: How does a red blood cell differ from a typical cell?
options: C) It has no nucleus — maximises space for haemoglobin — carries oxygen
questions.explanation: Red blood cells are enucleated (no nucleus) — biconcave shape maximises surface area for oxygen absorption.

questions.question: What happens during the cell cycle?
options: B) Cell growth, DNA replication (interphase) then cell division (mitosis)
questions.explanation: Cell cycle: interphase (cell grows, DNA replicates, organelles duplicate) → mitosis → cytokinesis (cells separate).

questions.question: How many chromosomes do human gametes contain?
questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid — 23 chromosomes. At fertilisation, 23 + 23 = 46 (diploid).

questions.question: Why are embryonic stem cells valuable in medicine?
options: A) They are easy to obtain
B) They can differentiate into any cell type — could replace damaged tissue
C) They only produce red blood cells
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — potential to treat conditions like Parkinson's, diabetes, and spinal injuries.

questions.question: What makes a sperm cell specialised?
options: C) Tail (flagellum) for movement, many mitochondria, acrosome with enzymes
questions.explanation: Sperm cells: tail for swimming, mitochondria for ATP, acrosome releases enzymes to penetrate the egg.

questions.question: Mitosis produces genetically identical cells.
questions.explanation: Mitosis is used for growth and repair — daughter cells identical to parent.

questions.question: Meiosis produces two daughter cells.
questions.explanation: Meiosis produces FOUR genetically different cells.

questions.question: Stem cells are highly specialised.
questions.explanation: Stem cells are UNDIFFERENTIATED — they can become specialised.

questions.question: Cancer is caused by uncontrolled cell division.
questions.explanation: Mutations in cell cycle control genes can lead to uncontrolled division.

questions.question: Human body cells are haploid.
questions.explanation: Human body cells are DIPLOID (2n = 46). Gametes are haploid (n = 23).

questions.question: Red blood cells have a nucleus.
questions.explanation: Mature red blood cells have NO nucleus — more space for haemoglobin.

questions.question: DNA replication occurs during interphase.
questions.explanation: During interphase, the cell grows and DNA is replicated before mitosis.

questions.question: Embryonic stem cells can differentiate into any cell type.
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent — potential to treat many conditions.

questions.question: Differentiation involves switching off all genes.
questions.explanation: Differentiation involves switching ON SPECIFIC genes — producing proteins for the cell's function.

questions.question: Gametes are produced by meiosis.
questions.explanation: Meiosis in reproductive organs produces haploid sperm and egg cells.

questions.question: ________ produces two identical cells — for growth and repair.
questions.explanation: Used in asexual reproduction too.

questions.question: ________ produces four different gametes — halves chromosome number.
questions.explanation: Gametes are haploid (n = 23 in humans).

questions.question: ________ is when cells become specialised by activating specific genes.
questions.explanation: Different cell types have different genes switched on.

questions.question: ________ cells are undifferentiated — can divide and become specialised.
questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells are pluripotent.

questions.question: ________ is uncontrolled cell division — caused by mutations.
questions.explanation: Mutations in cell cycle control genes allow uncontrolled division.

questions.question: ________ cells have two sets of chromosomes — all human body cells.
questions.explanation: 2n = 46 in humans.

questions.question: ________ cells have one set of chromosomes — gametes.
questions.explanation: n = 23 in humans.

questions.question: During ________, cells grow, DNA replicates, and organelles duplicate.
questions.explanation: Interphase is the longest part of the cell cycle.

questions.question: Red blood cells have no ________ — maximising space for haemoglobin.
questions.explanation: Their biconcave shape also increases surface area.

questions.question: ________ cells are pluripotent — valuable for treating disease.
questions.explanation: Could replace damaged cells in conditions like Parkinson's and diabetes.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: two identical cells — growth and repair
four different gametes — halves chromosome number
cells become specialised — specific genes activated
undifferentiated — can become specialised
uncontrolled cell division — mutations in cell cycle genes

cards.definition: brain, spinal cord, nerves — rapid electrical signals using neurones
a chemical messenger secreted by a gland — transported in the blood — slower but longer-lasting than nerves
maintaining a constant internal environment — temperature, blood glucose, water levels
a response that reverses a change — restores the set point
a microorganism that causes disease — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists
white blood cells destroy pathogens — phagocytosis, antibody production, memory cells
contains weakened or dead pathogens — stimulates immune response without causing disease
a drug that kills or inhibits bacteria — does not affect viruses
insulin (lowers) and glucagon (raises) — secreted by the pancreas
control of body temperature — hypothalamus coordinates sweating, shivering, vasodilation

questions.question: What is homeostasis?
options: B) Maintaining a constant internal environment
C) Responding to external stimuli only
questions.explanation: Homeostasis keeps conditions stable for enzyme function — temperature, blood glucose, water content.

questions.question: What is a pathogen?
options: C) A microorganism that causes disease — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists
questions.explanation: Pathogens cause disease by invading tissues, reproducing rapidly, and producing toxins.

questions.question: How do vaccines work?
options: B) They contain weakened/dead pathogens — stimulate immune response and memory cells
D) They replace damaged white blood cells
questions.explanation: Vaccination: immune system produces antibodies against the pathogen — memory cells remain for future rapid response.

questions.question: What is the role of insulin?
options: C) Lowers blood glucose — causes cells to absorb glucose
questions.explanation: Insulin is released when blood glucose is too high — causes cells to absorb glucose, liver to store it as glycogen.

questions.question: Why don't antibiotics work against viruses?
options: A) They do — antibiotics kill all pathogens
B) Antibiotics target bacterial structures — viruses use the host cell's machinery, not their own
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target specific bacterial processes (e.g. cell wall synthesis) — viruses don't have these structures.

questions.question: What is negative feedback?
options: A) A response that amplifies change
B) A mechanism that reverses changes to restore normal levels
C) A type of immune response
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: deviation detected → corrective response → return to set point.

questions.question: What is the immune response?
options: B) White blood cells destroying pathogens — phagocytosis, antibodies, memory cells
questions.explanation: Immune response: phagocytes engulf pathogens; lymphocytes produce antibodies; memory cells remain for future exposure.

questions.question: What is thermoregulation?
options: B) Control of body temperature — coordinated by the hypothalamus
questions.explanation: Thermoregulation: too hot → sweating, vasodilation; too cold → shivering, vasoconstriction. Hypothalamus coordinates.

questions.question: What is a hormone?
options: B) A chemical messenger in the blood — slower but longer-lasting than nerve signals
questions.explanation: Hormones are secreted by endocrine glands into the blood — they travel to target organs and produce longer-lasting effects.

questions.question: What is glucagon?
options: A) A hormone that lowers blood glucose
B) An enzyme in the liver
C) A hormone that raises blood glucose — stimulates glycogen breakdown
questions.explanation: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is too low — stimulates the liver to break down glycogen to glucose.

questions.question: Homeostasis maintains a stable internal environment.
questions.explanation: Negative feedback mechanisms keep temperature, blood glucose, and water content stable.

questions.question: Antibiotics cure viral infections.
questions.explanation: Antibiotics ONLY work against bacteria — not viruses.

questions.question: Vaccines contain live, fully virulent pathogens.
questions.explanation: Vaccines contain weakened, dead, or fragments of pathogens — safe but stimulate immunity.

questions.question: Insulin lowers blood glucose.
questions.explanation: Insulin causes cells to absorb glucose and the liver to convert glucose to glycogen.

questions.question: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is too high.
questions.explanation: Glucagon is released when blood glucose is TOO LOW — it raises glucose.

questions.question: Negative feedback amplifies changes.
questions.explanation: Negative feedback REVERSES changes — restoring the set point.

questions.question: Hormones are transported in the blood.
questions.explanation: Hormones travel in the bloodstream to target organs.

questions.question: The hypothalamus controls thermoregulation.
questions.explanation: The hypothalamus detects temperature changes and coordinates corrective responses.

questions.question: Memory cells from vaccination allow faster responses to future infections.
questions.explanation: Memory cells recognise the pathogen rapidly — producing antibodies faster on re-exposure.

questions.question: Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, and protists.
questions.explanation: All four groups contain organisms that can cause disease.

questions.question: ________ maintains a constant internal environment — uses negative feedback.
questions.explanation: Temperature, blood glucose, and water content are all regulated.

questions.question: ________ feedback reverses changes — restoring the set point.
questions.explanation: The basis of all homeostatic control mechanisms.

questions.question: ________ is a microorganism that causes disease — bacteria, viruses, fungi, protists.
questions.explanation: Pathogens invade tissues, reproduce, and produce toxins.

questions.question: ________ stimulate the immune system without causing disease.
questions.explanation: Memory cells remain — faster response on future exposure.

questions.question: ________ lowers blood glucose — secreted by the pancreas.
questions.explanation: Causes glucose uptake by cells and glycogen storage in liver.

questions.question: ________ raises blood glucose by stimulating glycogen breakdown.
questions.explanation: Released when blood glucose falls below the set point.

questions.question: ________ are chemical messengers transported in the blood — slower than nerve signals.
questions.explanation: Target specific organs — effects are longer-lasting.

questions.question: ________ kill bacteria — do not work against viruses.
questions.explanation: Antibiotics target bacterial structures absent in viruses.

questions.question: The ________ coordinates thermoregulation — detects temperature changes.
questions.explanation: Too hot: sweating and vasodilation. Too cold: shivering and vasoconstriction.

questions.question: The immune response includes phagocytosis, antibody production, and ________ cells.
questions.explanation: Memory cells remain after infection for rapid future responses.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: constant internal environment — negative feedback
microorganism causing disease — bacteria, virus, fungi, protist
weakened/dead pathogen — stimulates immune response
lowers blood glucose — pancreas
kill bacteria — not effective against viruses

cards.definition: all living organisms in an area and their non-living environment
multiple interconnected food chains — more realistic representation of feeding relationships
6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — plants make glucose using light energy
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + ATP — releasing energy from glucose
a section of DNA coding for a protein — carried on chromosomes
a version of a gene — dominant (expressed if present) or recessive (needs two copies)
organisms with advantageous adaptations survive and reproduce — alleles become more common
change in allele frequencies in a population over many generations
the variety of species — high biodiversity increases ecosystem stability
permanent loss of a species — caused by habitat loss, competition, disease, climate change

questions.question: What is photosynthesis?
options: B) 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — making glucose from CO₂ and water using light
C) Breaking down food in the gut
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts — light energy converts CO₂ and water to glucose and oxygen.

questions.question: What is the equation for aerobic respiration?
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration releases energy from glucose — produces ATP, CO₂, and water.

questions.question: What is natural selection?
options: A) Humans choosing which organisms breed
C) Organisms with advantageous adaptations surviving and reproducing — passing on beneficial alleles
D) All organisms adapting over one generation
questions.explanation: Natural selection: variation exists → some survive better → they reproduce more → beneficial alleles increase in frequency.

questions.question: What is evolution?
options: B) Change in allele frequencies in a population over many generations
questions.explanation: Evolution is driven by natural selection — allele frequencies change over many generations, leading to new adaptations.

questions.question: What is biodiversity?
options: A) Number of individuals in one species
B) Variety of different species in an ecosystem
questions.explanation: High biodiversity = more stable ecosystems. Biodiversity is threatened by habitat loss, pollution, and climate change.

questions.question: What is a food web?
options: B) Multiple interconnected food chains — realistic feeding relationships
C) A diagram of all organisms in an ecosystem
questions.explanation: Food webs show the complexity of feeding relationships — more accurately representing real ecosystems than single food chains.

questions.question: What is the difference between gene and allele?
options: B) A gene is a section of DNA coding for a protein; an allele is a version of that gene
C) Alleles are found on different chromosomes
D) Genes are found in mitochondria
questions.explanation: Gene = a section of DNA coding for a specific protein. Allele = a variant of that gene at the same locus.

questions.question: What can cause extinction?
options: B) Habitat loss, competition, disease, climate change — species permanently lost
questions.explanation: Extinction is permanent — causes include habitat destruction, introduced species, disease, climate change, and overexploitation.

questions.question: What is the role of decomposers in an ecosystem?
options: C) Breaking down dead matter — returning nutrients to the ecosystem
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria and fungi) break down dead organic matter — releasing nutrients that plants can absorb.

questions.question: What is respiration?
options: B) Releasing energy from glucose — produces ATP, CO₂, and water
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration occurs in mitochondria — it releases energy from glucose to produce ATP for cell processes.

questions.question: Photosynthesis produces glucose and oxygen.
questions.explanation: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — in chloroplasts.

questions.question: Aerobic respiration produces lactic acid.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration produces CO₂ and water. ANAEROBIC respiration produces lactic acid (in animals).

questions.question: Natural selection requires organisms to consciously adapt.
questions.explanation: Natural selection is not conscious — organisms with existing advantageous variations survive and reproduce more.

questions.question: Extinction is reversible.
questions.explanation: Extinction is PERMANENT — once a species is gone, it cannot return.

questions.question: High biodiversity makes ecosystems more stable.
questions.explanation: More species = more feeding relationships = more resilience to change.

questions.question: A gene and an allele are the same thing.
questions.explanation: A GENE codes for a protein. An ALLELE is a specific version of that gene.

questions.question: Decomposers break down dead matter and return nutrients.
questions.explanation: Without decomposers, nutrients would not be recycled and ecosystems would collapse.

questions.question: Evolution occurs over a single generation.
questions.explanation: Evolution requires MANY generations — allele frequencies change slowly over time.

questions.question: Food webs are less realistic than food chains.
questions.explanation: Food webs are MORE realistic — they show multiple interconnected feeding relationships.

questions.question: Respiration occurs in mitochondria.
questions.explanation: Aerobic respiration occurs in mitochondria — producing ATP from glucose.

questions.question: ________ = 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — making glucose using light.
questions.explanation: Occurs in chloroplasts.

questions.question: Aerobic ________ releases energy from glucose — produces ATP, CO₂, water.
questions.explanation: Occurs in mitochondria.

questions.question: ________ selection: organisms with advantageous adaptations survive and reproduce more.
questions.explanation: Beneficial alleles increase in frequency over generations.

questions.question: ________ is change in allele frequencies over many generations.
questions.explanation: Driven by natural selection acting on variation.

questions.question: ________ is the variety of species — high levels increase stability.
questions.explanation: Threatened by habitat loss, pollution, and climate change.

questions.question: ________ break down dead matter — returning nutrients to the ecosystem.
questions.explanation: Bacteria and fungi are the main decomposers.

questions.question: A ________ is a section of DNA coding for a specific protein.
questions.explanation: Genes are found at specific locations (loci) on chromosomes.

questions.question: An ________ is a version of a gene — dominant or recessive.
questions.explanation: Alleles at the same locus on homologous chromosomes.

questions.question: ________ is the permanent loss of a species.
questions.explanation: Cannot be reversed — caused by habitat loss, disease, climate change.

questions.question: A food ________ shows multiple interconnected feeding relationships.
questions.explanation: More realistic than a single food chain.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ — light energy
C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + ATP
advantageous adaptations → survive → reproduce → alleles spread
change in allele frequencies over generations
variety of species — high = more stable

blocks.block_name: Separate Science: Plant Biology & Genetics in Depth

cards.definition: loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata — drives water up the xylem
pores in leaves for gas exchange — surrounded by guard cells that control opening
cells that control stomatal opening — swell when turgid (stomata open) or shrink (stomata close)
vascular tissue transporting water and minerals from roots to leaves — dead cells with lignified walls
vascular tissue transporting dissolved sugars (e.g. sucrose) — from leaves to all parts of the plant
transport of sucrose through phloem — from source (leaves) to sink (roots, growing tips)
when both alleles in a heterozygote are expressed — neither is dominant — e.g. blood group AB
genes carried on sex chromosomes — e.g. colour blindness carried on the X chromosome
inserting a gene from one organism into another — e.g. human insulin gene into bacteria
choosing organisms with desirable characteristics to breed — increases frequency of those traits

questions.question: What is transpiration?
options: A) Transport of sucrose through phloem
B) Loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata
C) Absorption of water by roots
questions.explanation: Transpiration: water evaporates from leaves → creates a 'pull' that draws water up through the xylem from roots.

questions.question: What controls stomatal opening?
options: C) Guard cells — swelling (opening) or shrinking (closing) by osmosis
questions.explanation: Guard cells control stomata: when turgid (full of water) they curve — opening the stomatal pore. When flaccid, they close.

questions.question: What is codominance?
options: A) One allele always masks the other
B) Both alleles in a heterozygote are expressed — e.g. blood group AB
D) A type of sex-linked inheritance
questions.explanation: Codominance: both alleles contribute to the phenotype. Blood group AB: both A and B antigens expressed.

questions.question: What is sex-linked inheritance?
options: A) All genes on X and Y chromosomes
B) Genes carried on sex chromosomes — e.g. colour blindness on the X chromosome
D) Genes only affecting one sex
questions.explanation: Sex-linked genes are on the sex chromosomes — mostly on X. Males (XY) only have one copy, so recessive X-linked traits more commonly affect males.

questions.question: What is genetic engineering?
options: B) Inserting a gene from one organism into another to produce a desired protein
questions.explanation: Genetic engineering: e.g. human insulin gene inserted into bacteria → bacteria produce human insulin for diabetes treatment.

questions.question: What is translocation in plants?
options: C) Transport of sucrose through phloem — from source to sink
D) Movement of minerals by active transport
questions.explanation: Translocation moves organic molecules (sucrose, amino acids) through the phloem — from where they are made (source) to where needed (sink).

questions.question: What is selective breeding?
options: A) Natural selection in the wild
C) Choosing organisms with desired traits to breed — increasing frequency of those traits
questions.explanation: Selective breeding has been used for thousands of years — producing high-yield crops, disease-resistant plants, and domesticated animals.

questions.question: What is the function of xylem?
options: B) Transport of water and minerals from roots to leaves
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are dead cells with lignified walls — they transport water by the cohesion-tension mechanism.

questions.question: What is the function of phloem?
options: A) Transport of water from roots
C) Transport of dissolved sugars from leaves to all parts of the plant
questions.explanation: Phloem is made of living cells — it transports sucrose and other organic molecules by translocation.

questions.question: What does it mean when colour blindness is X-linked?
options: A) Only females can be colour blind
B) The gene is on the X chromosome — males with one copy show the trait
C) Colour blindness affects X chromosomes
D) X chromosomes cause colour blindness
questions.explanation: X-linked recessive traits: females need two copies to be affected (can be carriers); males only need one copy — more commonly affected.

questions.question: Transpiration is loss of water vapour through stomata.
questions.explanation: Transpiration drives water movement up through the xylem from roots to leaves.

questions.question: Guard cells close stomata by becoming turgid.
questions.explanation: Guard cells OPEN stomata when turgid (swell). They CLOSE when flaccid (shrink).

questions.question: Codominance means only one allele is expressed.
questions.explanation: Codominance means BOTH alleles are expressed in the heterozygote.

questions.question: Sex-linked genes are only on the Y chromosome.
questions.explanation: Sex-linked genes are mostly on the X chromosome. Males (XY) only have one X — so recessive traits are more common in males.

questions.question: Genetic engineering involves selective breeding.
questions.explanation: Genetic engineering inserts genes from one species into another. Selective breeding works within a species.

questions.question: Translocation moves water through the xylem.
questions.explanation: Translocation moves SUCROSE through the PHLOEM. Water moves through the xylem.

questions.question: Xylem transports water and minerals from roots to leaves.
questions.explanation: Xylem vessels are dead cells with lignified walls — efficient water transport.

questions.question: Selective breeding uses genetic engineering techniques.
questions.explanation: Selective breeding is traditional — choosing parents with desired traits. Genetic engineering is modern — directly manipulating DNA.

questions.question: Phloem transports dissolved sugars from source to sink.
questions.explanation: Translocation through phloem: from leaves (source) to roots, growing tips (sinks).

questions.question: Males are more commonly affected by X-linked recessive conditions.
questions.explanation: Males only have one X chromosome — one copy of a recessive allele is enough to show the trait.

questions.question: ________ is loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata.
questions.explanation: Creates tension that pulls water up through xylem.

questions.question: ________ cells control stomatal opening — open when turgid, close when flaccid.
questions.explanation: Stomata control gas exchange and water loss.

questions.question: ________ is when both alleles are expressed in a heterozygote — e.g. blood group AB.
questions.explanation: Neither allele is dominant — both contribute to phenotype.

questions.question: ________ inheritance: genes on sex chromosomes — colour blindness on X chromosome.
questions.explanation: Males more commonly affected — only one X chromosome.

questions.question: ________ engineering: inserting a gene from one organism into another.
questions.explanation: E.g. human insulin gene in bacteria for diabetes treatment.

questions.question: ________ is transport of sucrose through phloem — from source to sink.
questions.explanation: Source: leaves. Sinks: roots, growing tips, flowers.

questions.question: ________ transports water and minerals from roots to leaves.
questions.explanation: Dead cells with lignified walls — cohesion-tension mechanism.

questions.question: ________ transports dissolved sugars from leaves to all parts of the plant.
questions.explanation: Living cells — translocation driven by active loading.

questions.question: ________ breeding selects organisms with desired traits to breed — traditional.
questions.explanation: Used for thousands of years — dogs, crops, livestock.

questions.question: X-linked recessive traits are more common in ________ — they only have one X.
questions.explanation: Females can be carriers with one recessive allele.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: water vapour loss from stomata — drives xylem flow
water and mineral transport — root to leaf
sucrose transport — source to sink — translocation
both alleles expressed in heterozygote
gene from one organism inserted into another
